text.title: Venn Model: Overlapping Roles in AI-Era Product Teams
text.summary: Describes the shift from relay-race handoffs to overlapping Venn diagram teams, the new PM skillset required in 2026, and cross-functional expectations per role.

section.title: Traditional vs. AI-Era PM Skillset
section.p: Traditional PM skills: write specs, own roadmap, manage stakeholders, prioritize features. AI-era PM additionally requires: speak design fluently, understand data deeply (write SQL), know how models work, have a credible view on distribution and pricing strategy.

rule.statement: Write every PM job description as "primary lens (product strategy) + 3 fluencies (data, design, AI) + 1 specialist depth + explicit non-goals" rather than a flat skill list.
rule.rationale: A flat skill list produces shallow generalism and fails to encode where the PM does NOT own depth; the T-shape with explicit non-goals prevents PM burnout from absorbing every discipline.

section.title: Team Ratio Evolution
section.p: Traditional ratio: 1 PM to 4-8 engineers. AI-era ratio: 2 PMs to 1 engineer (Andrew Ng observation). This holds only when the engineer is heavily AI-augmented — track agent-assisted velocity separately before changing the ratio. The bottleneck shifts to discovery, evals, and prioritization — all PM work.

rule.statement: Treat the 2:1 PM:Engineer ratio as a hypothesis per team, not an industry benchmark; validate against actual agent-assisted engineering throughput before any hiring decision.
rule.rationale: The ratio was one observation from one operator; applying it without measuring current agent-assisted velocity risks over-hiring PMs and under-building the product.

section.title: Cross-Functional Expectations
section.p: Role expectations in AI-era teams: PM — speak design, understand data, know AI basics; Designer — understand constraints, track activation metrics, own one funnel; Engineer — join discovery, talk to customers; Data — participate in product decisions, not just supply numbers.

rule.statement: Pair every "PM speaks design" expectation with a designer-side blurring expectation; one-sided blurring breeds resentment and burns out the PM.
rule.rationale: The Venn model describes mutual overlap, not PM absorption of all disciplines; unilateral blurring turns the PM into a permanent integrator with no leverage.

antipattern.description: Full blurring expectations on deep domains (security, accessibility, ML eval, monetization) with no specialist backstop.
antipattern.reason: Full blurring is an anti-pattern in deep domains; keep one depth specialist PM per critical surface as the audit backstop — the Venn model does not mean every PM must be equally broad in every area.

antipattern.description: Using the Venn model as a hiring filter that shuts out specialists (deep researcher, deep platform PM, deep growth PM).
antipattern.reason: Skill-blurring expectations in JDs covertly reject candidates the team actually needs; label specialist roles explicitly rather than requiring generalism that doesn't exist in the market.
